  • Perdoceo Education Corporation (PRDO);
  • Laureate Education, Inc. (LAUR);
  • American Public Education, Inc. (APEI).

Starting with the P/E, we note that the average of these five companies gives us an average multiple of 16.3x. Analysts expect EPS of $5.51 to $5.72 (consensus at $5.63) for the full FY 2025. This would give us a price target of approximately $91.80 for this stock.

Moving on to another equity multiple, the P/S of the comparable companies is 2.6x. Analysts estimate FY 2025 revenue between $1.26 billion and $1.28 billion (consensus $1.27 billion). With roughly 22 million shares outstanding at the end of FY 2025, I expect an RPS of $57.72. That gives us a price target of $150.

Sounds pretty high, doesn’t it? Excluding outliers like Grand Canyon, we have a 2x multiple, which gives us a price target of $115.

Jumping to EV multiples, let’s take a look at EV/EBIT. First, let’s assume an EBIT margin of 10% to 14%. With revenues estimated at $1.27 billion, EBIT would be projected at $127 to $177 million. Other assumptions we could make are about net debt. I expect them to be sitting on at least $200 million in cash and equivalents at the end of FY 2025, with that being negative net debt. And of course, 22 million shares outstanding at the end of the year.

The average comparable multiple for EV/EBIT is 12.3x. Using the low end of the EBIT estimates, we would have a price target of $80—very close to where the stock is trading today. Now, if we consider the high-end, we would have a price target of around $108.

Considering all metrics, we have the following price target perspective for Strategic Education:

A Headwind to Consider: Australia and New Zealand

Revenues from Australia and New Zealand make up around 20% of the company’s total—but that chunk is under some pressure right now.

But why? Simple. Changes in the regulatory environment. Let me explain.

Anthony Albanese, Australia’s Prime Minister, assumed office with a commitment to implementing stricter controls on international student admissions—especially at Aussie universities, where a large share of students come from nearby China. Now, a coalition is pushing to cap international enrollments at 240,000 per year.

That’s not great news for Strategic Education, especially considering they entered the region just five years ago. A large part of their student base there tends to be international, so this policy shift could hit them where it hurts.

Strategic will focus more on Australian and New Zealand students, but we can safely expect enrollments to decline by low to mid-single digits for the rest of the year.

Bottom Line

Putting it all together, we’re looking at a potential upside of 26.5% just from capital gains if the stock hits my $105 price target. Now tack on another 10% or so from dividends and buybacks, and you’ve got a total return approaching 40% by the end of 2025.

With tailwinds from the EdTech segment (particularly from WorkForce Edge partnerships), we have a strong outlook for FY 2025. And perhaps best of all: a squeaky-clean balance sheet capable of both M&A and returning capital to shareholders.

That said, I’m starting my coverage of Strategic Education with a ‘Strong Buy’ rating.

data.title: NYLI Epoch Capital Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The MSCI World Index fell 1.7% over the first quarter of 2025. Equities sold off in the second half of Q1 as enthusiasm faded post Trump's inauguration, replaced instead by unease arising from tariff uncertainty, creeping doubts in the Artificial Intelligence (AI) secular growth story fueled by the release of DeepSeek, softer economic data, and depressed sentiment. Cyclical pockets expressed this hit, with discretionary industries underperforming many defensives. Caution was further indicated by the various downward revisions of growth forecasts by corporate and central banks alike, coupled with negative earnings revisions. The American stock market was the main laggard this quarter as the megacap tech names pulled back on reduced faith in the payoff of AI capex spending. U.S. markets were further plagued by tariff and policy uncertainty, which caused some rotation to favor ex-U.S. investments as well as revision upward of inflation expectations. Europe outperformed, particularly with the added boosts of German expansionary fiscal impulse, accommodative central banks, and a rally by European defense stocks in anticipation of increased military spending. However, European economic indicators remain sluggish, with muted growth forecasts and continued monetary easing attempting to combat it. Asian equity performance was more of a mixed bag over the quarter, with China and Hong Kong the standouts and Japan, Taiwan, and South Korea trailing behind. China and Hong Kong indices were particularly propelled by technology boons like DeepSeek's announced AI advancements and fiscal support for the sector. The region's economic data, however, reflected a slight claw back in retail sales and industrial output, but continues to show labor market weakness. The top performing sectors this quarter included energy (+10.1%), utilities (+7.2%), and financials (+6.1%).

Information technology (-11.9%), consumer discretionary (-10.3%), and communications services (-4.4%) all lagged this quarter due in part to the correction in the Magnificent 7** names.

**(Alphabet (Google)(GOOG)(GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta platforms (Facebook) (META), Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA) & Tesla (TSLA))

Portfolio Highlights

  • The NYLI Epoch Capital Growth Fund (the “Fund”) underperformed its benchmark by ~1.0% in the first quarter. This consisted of a negative impact from factor exposures, that was partially offset by a positive contribution from stock-specific risk. Industry and style factors both detracted, while country and currency exposures contributed positively.
  • Over the course of the quarter, the trades we made, together with the impact of the varying performance across stocks and sectors, caused our weights in the health care and consumer staples sectors to rise by 2.5% and 0.7%, respectively. Meanwhile, our weight in the information technology sector fell by 2.0% and our weight in consumer discretionary fell by 1.5%. As of March 31st, our biggest overweight was in health care (+8.2%), followed by consumer discretionary (6.7%) and industrials (+4.5%). We were underweight in almost every other sector, led by financials (-6.0%) and information technology (-4.3%), followed by utilities (-2.7%), real estate (-2.2%), communication services (-2.1%), and materials (-1.7%). As we always note, none of the sector weightings reflect a top-down view, but instead result from the individual holdings identified by our investment process, together with the overall risk management that takes place during portfolio construction.
  • On the other hand, something that very much is intentional is the Fund's wide Return on Invested Capital - Weighted Average Cost of Capital (ROIC-WACC) spread compared to the benchmark. As of March 31st, the Fund's weighted-median aggregate spread was 18.4%, compared to 7.8% for the MSCI World Index.

What Helped:

  • Looking at country and currency exposures, the underweight to the United States helped performance amid the sell-off, in addition to overweights to Spain and China. The underweight to Germany was the top detracting country exposure as the administration kicked up fiscal support for the economy. The overweights to the Swedish krona, Mexican peso, and British pound contributed to active returns as well.
  • Positively contributing industry exposures included the overweight to Health Care Providers & Services and underweights to Technology Hardware and Software. The positive exposure to profitability contributed 0.2% in active return.
  • As for stock-specific risk, BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), Chemed (CHE), BayCurrent (OTCPK:BYCRF), and Yum Brands (YUM) all contributed positively to active performance during Q1, coupled with the portfolio's underweight positions in the Magnificent 7. BYD released a few positive announcements in February that boosted its share price, including strong unit sales growth and the advancement of its AI-powered smart driving system. The company also continues to enjoy the windfalls of competitive pricing and favorable valuation, making it a preferred source of Electric Vehicle (EV) exposure over peers.

What Hurt:

  • Industry exposures collectively detracted 1.5%. The underweights to Aerospace & Defense and Insurance, in addition to the overweight to Life Sciences Tools & Services, inflicted the largest negative impact on active return. The underweight to Banks also detracted. Within styles, negative exposures to momentum and value detracted, along with the positive market sensitivity exposure. Other all, The underweights to the Japanese yen and euro detracted from active returns.
  • The stocks that caused the largest detractions were Deckers Outdoor Corporation (DECK), eMemory, Arista Networks (ANET), and West Pharmaceutical (WST). Deckers shares have been under pressure year-to-date, largely reflecting fiscal fourth quarter guidance offered at the end of January that was more cautious than the market had anticipated. We believe the lower-than-expected guidance is largely a function of the timing of new product launches and suggests a lower growth rate than the company has had in recent years. An uncertain consumer environment and the prospect of tariffs may have also weighed on shares in the near-term. We continue to have faith in the company due to the Hoka brand's robust innovation slate to be launched through 2025, meaningful international expansion opportunities, and healthy profitability in the form of high ROIC.

Top Contributors and Detractors (%)

Outlook and Positioning:

  • Equity markets began the year on a positive note, but the trend changed dramatically from February 19th as the focus shifted to trade wars. This weakness was initially concentrated in U.S. markets but has now spread to all major equity markets globally.
  • Overall, we believe the trade war will proceed in three stages, beginning with tariff hikes. The second stage, which we've just entered, is about negotiating deals, with the stock market likely to bottom once real progress is signaled. On the other hand, lack of progress means the "America alone" scenario and suggests further downside. The third stage, likely to begin in Q3, emphasizes Trump's pro-growth agenda, which includes tax cuts, deregulation, shrinking the government, and pursing pro-tech policies. If this, admittedly optimistic, scenario is realized, equities could be significantly above today's level by year-end.
  • Turning to market fundamentals, consensus earnings forecasts do not yet adequately incorporate recent developments in the trade war. For example, the S&P 500 Index is still expected to grow Earnings Per Share (EPS) this year by 12% (marked down from 15% last month) and 14% (unchanged) in 2026. The corresponding EPS growth estimates are slightly lower for Europe (7% and 11%), Japan (10% and 9%) and China (8% and 12%), with these forecasts only revised marginally lower over the last month. It is highly likely that consensus 2025 EPS growth estimates will be slashed over coming months.
  • Regarding valuations, the 2025 12-month Forward Price Earnings ratio for the S&P 500 Index is currently 19x, just slightly above the historical mean of 17x. Similarly, the tech sector is now trading at 23x, down from 29x two months ago. Markets outside the U.S. look quite attractive, with Europe at 14x, Japan 13x, and China 12x. All are still trading at exceptionally large discounts to the U.S.
  • What does all this mean for the equity market outlook? Trade war induced uncertainty is likely to be with us for a few more months, if not quarters. However, Trump 2.0 tax cuts, a pro-tech bias and deregulation measures, as well as the "Fed put," amount to powerful reasons to expect the S&P 500 Index to appreciate by 10% before year-end.

The opinions expressed are those of Epoch Investment Partners, Inc. as of the date of this report and are subject to change. There is no guarantee that any forecast made will come to pass. data.title: Reserve Bank Of Australia Preview: Next Week Provides Another Window To Cut Rates
data.description:
By Deepali Bhargava & Francesco Pesole

Likely to be another high conviction rate cut

We expect the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) to cut the cash rate by 25bp to 3.6% at the upcoming policy meeting next week. Recent data flows on growth and inflation have been softer than expected.

The previous rate adjustment by the RBA was not only a reduction but a decisive one, with discussions centred around whether the cut should be 25bp or 50bp. In alignment with other central banks in the region, the RBA adopted a dovish stance on the growth trajectory due to tariff uncertainties and also revised its inflation forecasts downward.

Weaker growth and softer inflation reinforce our view

What adds conviction to our call next week is a much softer-than-expected CPI inflation reading for May. Headline CPI decreased from 2.4% in April 2025 to 2.1% in May 2025, nearing the lower end of the central bank's target range of 2-3%. High trimmed mean inflation reflecting high services inflation, which has been a more persistent issue, also declined to 2.4% year-over-year, indicating substantial progress in controlling inflation from a peak of 4.4% year-over-year one year ago.

First-quarter GDP growth was weak too, with quarter-on-quarter growth at just 0.2%, falling below expectations. Softer private consumption growth was partly seasonal and due to extreme weather events, but public spending has had the largest negative impact on growth since the September quarter of 2017, and net trade also dragged GDP growth lower.

However, employment data was strong, with wage growth rising to a solid 3.4% year-over-year in the first quarter. In May 2025, despite a small drop in overall job growth, full-time employment rose by about 39,000, which partly offset losses in part-time employment. Reduced underemployment and strong demand for hours worked also point to a healthy labour market.

Adding one more rate cut to the 2025 profile

Overall, the global backdrop on tariffs remains uncertain, and both headline and trimmed inflation have been firmly on a downtrend, remaining close to the midpoint of the 2-3% range over the next few quarters.

The labour data could start to turn as well. In the previous monetary policy statement, members observed that the rate of voluntary job turnover had declined and that, anecdotally, the focus of wage bargaining and employment disputes had tilted in favour of workers seeking greater job security. This might see wage growth slow more noticeably than currently forecast.

Given that the downside risks to both growth and inflation are more dominant at this stage, we are adding a 25bp rate cut to our 2025 profile, taking rates to a terminal level of 3.1% by the end of 2025.

AUD/USD may be close to a peak

Risks for AUD around the 9 July US tariff deadline are no greater than for other high-beta currencies, and certainly not more significant than for USD.

AUD has experienced the largest drop in short-term swap rates across G10 since the start of the year: 65bp in the two-year tenor. Although a host of external drivers has sometimes obscured the FX impact, the sensitivity of AUD to rate differentials has increased, as reflected in our G10 short-term valuation model. While this is primarily a function of Fed repricing, a quieter backdrop on tariffs – barring any major escalation in July – means central bank policy is regaining its place as the main driver of relative value in risk-exposed currencies.

AUD front-end swap rates have fallen the most in G10 this year

Markets are currently pricing in 17bp of easing for this month’s RBA meeting and 78bp by year-end. We believe a cut next week could trigger a broader dovish repricing along the AUD OIS curve, with a more visible impact on AUD. On this basis, we expect AUD to lag behind NZD and potentially other high-beta currencies where there is less room for front-end rates to fall, such as SEK.

AUD/USD came close to touching our near-term target of 0.660, with the move still almost entirely driven by US developments. With US inflation, in our view, set to rebound in the coming months and drive a hawkish repricing in USD rates, we expect a recovery in USD. Coupled with a more dovish RBA – though this remains a secondary driver – we expect AUD/USD to move back to 0.650 this summer.

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
